mappings.rationaleAI: Directly addresses the intellectual and institutional transformation of twelfth-century Western Europe: the founding of universities at Bologna, Paris, and Oxford (A.1.e.i), the recovery of Aristotle and development of Scholasticism (A.1.e.ii), and the emergence of Gothic architecture (A.1.e.iii).

The Dominican and Franciscan mendicant orders are central to A.1.d's reform movements and the Inquisition, while Aquinas's and Bonaventure's synthesis of Aristotelian philosophy with Christian theology at Paris and Oxford directly covers Scholasticism (A.1.e.ii).

Eleanor's marriages to Louis VII and Henry II link Capetian France (A.3.a) and early Plantagenet England (A.5.b.i), her vast Aquitaine inheritance set the stage for 250 years of Anglo-French territorial conflict, and her career illuminates the status of women in medieval society (A.1.a).
Traces the political theology of divine right from medieval sacral kingship, directly relevant to A.1.g.ii on kingship as the ideal Christian form of government and its relationship to sacrality.

Marsilius's Defensor Pacis (1324) arguing for popular sovereignty over papal temporal claims engages the relations between temporal and spiritual power (A.1.g.i), and his political theory on the decline of papal supremacy and rising national monarchies is central to late medieval intellectual developments (B.1.c).

The 1122 resolution of the Investiture Controversy is the defining event of A.2.a.i (the clash between papacy and emperors over lay investiture) and establishes the precedent for separating temporal and spiritual power central to A.1.g.i.

Venice's rise as a maritime power, its commercial expansion in the eastern Mediterranean, and its republican institutions map to Venetian maritime power in northern Italy (A.2.a.iii), expansion in the Levant including the Fourth Crusade (A.2.b.iv), and Venice's republican institutions and commercial empire in the late medieval period (B.5.b.iii, B.5.c.iv).

The Hanseatic confederation dominating North European and Baltic trade corresponds to the revival of commercial activity and towns (A.1.b), the development of commercial centres like Lubeck (A.2.b.i), its influence on Scandinavian economic developments (A.6.b), and German urban economy in the 14th-15th centuries (B.4.a.ii).
The civil war between Stephen and Matilda (1135-54) is the specific subject of A.5.a.iii, and the Treaty of Winchester establishing Henry II as successor directly founded the Plantagenet dynasty.

The 1086 Domesday survey commissioned by William the Conqueror is explicitly named in A.5.a.i and serves as a primary source for understanding the introduction of feudalism and royal administration under the Norman Conquest (A.5.a).

The 1217 battle during the First Barons' War follows directly from John's conflict with the barons and Magna Carta (A.5.b.iii), while William Marshal's consolidation of Henry III's rule and reissue of Magna Carta connects to the early reign of Henry III (A.5.b.iv).

Richard I's participation in the Third Crusade and administration during his absence are central to A.5.b.ii, while Frederick Barbarossa's drowning en route directly involves the Hohenstaufen emperor of A.2.b.i.

David I's introduction of feudal landholding, royal burghs, monasteries, and Anglo-Norman administrative structures constitutes the formative period of Scottish monarchy covered in A.5.c.i (unification of Scotland and development of the monarchy).

The Teutonic Order's crusading campaigns in northeastern Europe and founding of a state in Prussia are directly relevant to the early role of the Teutonic Order in eastern Europe (A.7.a) and the broader context of Poland-Lithuania and the Teutonic Order (B.10.b).

The episode spans Bohemia's full medieval arc: the Premysl rulers (A.7.b), Prague under Charles IV as the Luxemburg dynasty's capital (B.4.b.i), Jan Hus's reform movement and execution at Constance (B.4.b.ii), and the subsequent Hussite Wars (B.4.b.iii).

The Commonwealth's medieval origins through dynastic ties between Poland and Lithuania in the fourteenth century, its elective monarchy, and its relationship to the Teutonic Order correspond to B.10.b on Poland-Lithuania and the broader eastern European political developments.

The revival of Greco-Roman mythology in Renaissance art, from Botticelli's Primavera to papal patronage of pagan-themed art in the Vatican, covers the relationship between Humanism and Christianity (B.1.a.i) and new concepts in painting and sculpture (B.1.a.ii).

Langland's c.1370 alliterative poem exploring social justice, clerical corruption, and moral crisis in post-plague England exemplifies late Gothic literary culture in northern Europe (B.1.b), late medieval intellectual developments (B.1.c), and the social upheaval following the Black Death (B.2.b).

The 1347-50 pandemic and its economic, social, and religious consequences are the defining event of B.2.b (effects of the Black Death and subsequent epidemics), and the demographic catastrophe arriving via Genoese ships at Messina connects to the crises of the 14th century in Italy (B.5.a).

The 1346 Battle of Crecy with its decisive role of the longbow against mounted knights exemplifies the declining importance of feudal nobility through changing military technology (B.2.a) and is a major engagement in the early phase of the Hundred Years' War (B.6.a.ii).

Joan of Arc's 1429 relief of Orleans, the subsequent coronation of Charles VII at Reims, and the stirring of French national feeling are the central events of B.6.a.v (recovery, reunification, and expulsion of the English).

The Medici family's dominance of Florence, their banking wealth, and patronage of Botticelli, Michelangelo, and Leonardo address the crisis of Florentine republicanism and Medici rule (B.5.c.i) and the contribution of city-states to Renaissance developments in the fine arts (B.1.a, B.1.a.ii).

mappings.episodeTitle: Machiavelli and the Italian City States
mappings.rationaleAI: Machiavelli's political realism and The Prince arise from the context of Renaissance Florence, papal politics, and foreign invasions, covering late medieval political theory (B.1.c) and the Italian states in the 15th century (B.5.c).

Glyndwr's major revolt against English rule under Henry IV, his establishment of a Welsh parliament, and the eventual English reconquest connect directly to fluctuating English influence in Wales (B.7.c).

Margaret's political and military leadership during Henry VI's incapacity and her role as a catalyst for the Wars of the Roses are central to B.7.a.iii (domestic rivalries and Wars of the Roses) and B.7.a.iv (the reigns of Edward IV and Richard III).

Islamic civilization in Iberia provides essential context for the Spanish Christian kingdoms' expansion into Muslim territory (A.4, A.4.b), and the fall of Granada under Ferdinand and Isabella in 1492 with the expulsion of Jews maps to B.8.c.i.

The 1478 tribunal established under Ferdinand and Isabella targeting converted Jews and Muslims is directly addressed in B.8.c.i (the Inquisition and treatment of Jews under the united Spanish monarchy).

The Battle of Agincourt (1415) occurred during the Hundred Years' War between England and France; Henry V's campaign through Normandy and the battle fall under the late medieval English kingdom (A.5.b.iv Henry III and Edward I) and especially late medieval developments including the Hundred Years' War period, and the broader context of Anglo-French conflict (A.5.a.iii later Capetians).
Robert the Bruce, Bannockburn (1314), and Scottish-English conflict are directly covered under the unification of Scotland and relations with the English crown (A.5.c.i).

Caxton operated in the late medieval period; the printing revolution relates to technological inventions and improvements (A.1.c), and his printing of works like Canterbury Tales connects to the early Renaissance in Italy and northern Europe (B.1.a.i revival of Greek studies and formation of Classical libraries, Humanism).

Chaucer wrote in the 14th century, part of the arts of the High Middle Ages including Latin and vernacular literature (A.1.e.iii), and his Continental influences (Dante, Boccaccio) connect to late medieval intellectual developments (B.1.c).

Christine de Pizan wrote at the French Court in the late Middle Ages; her work directly addresses the status of women in medieval Western European society (A.1.a) and contributes to vernacular literature of the High Middle Ages (A.1.e.iii).

Gerald served Henry II and was active in 12th-century Anglo-Norman England and its continental dependencies (A.5), during the high medieval period of Western Christendom's cultural and political life (A.1).

The play dramatizes late medieval English political conflict -- the rebellion against Henry IV -- set in the period of England from the Norman Conquest through the Plantagenet era (A.5) and England in the later Middle Ages (B.7).
Hildegard (1098-1179) was active during the high medieval period, corresponding with popes and emperors, within the society, economy, and culture of Western Christendom (A.1).

Malory composed the work in late 15th-century England (B.7 on England, Wales, Scotland, and Ireland c.1307-c.1500), drawing on high medieval chivalric culture (A.1 on society, economy, and culture of high medieval Western Europe).
Kempe (c.1373-c.1438) was active in late medieval England (B.7 on England c.1307-c.1500), with her pilgrimages reflecting the religious culture of Western Christendom (A.5).
The infrastructure of pilgrimage routes, hostels, and guidebooks from the 12th-15th centuries reflects the society, economy, and culture of high medieval Western Europe (A.1).
